This stage sits in the executable-validation layer of the system: after the apply-patch binary has been built, it exercises the program exactly as a user would invoke it and checks the resulting process behavior and filesystem state. Rather than testing parser internals or library helpers, it confirms the standalone executable’s external contract.

apply-patch/tests/all.rs is the single integration-test entry point, wiring Cargo’s test harness to the shared suite. apply-patch/tests/suite/mod.rs structures that suite into focused modules and applies platform gating so tests that depend on non-Windows behavior only run where valid.

Within the suite, cli.rs covers the basic command-line invocation paths, including positional arguments and stdin-fed patch input, and verifies visible success for simple add and update operations. tool.rs expands coverage to the full CLI contract: successful application, malformed input and parser failures, filesystem error cases, overwrite rules, rename handling, and partial-application outcomes. scenarios.rs provides the broadest regression net by replaying fixture-driven end-to-end cases against copied directory trees and comparing the final tree to an expected snapshot. Together, these tests ensure the executable behaves correctly across realistic user workflows.
